excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "Inspired by My Teammates." Tightening up after seeing my teammates in action. My time in Cebu is almost over! Up until now, I've been spending my February and April days off on FOMU
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"Inspired by My Teammates."

▼ Tightening Up After Seeing My Teammates in Action

My time in Cebu is almost over!

Up until now, I've been spending my days off in February and April

on FOMUS activities.

*March was for hanging out with school friends

Personally, I thought I'd been pretty active,

but then something happened

that made me think, "I need to do more!"

Kuma, a FOMUS member,

has been using crowdfunding

to do personal activities in Vietnam.

There, he's been spreading FOMUS masu in Vietnam too, and he's been scouting out local shops every single day.

I'd been connecting with local people through Masu Photo, but at some point I stopped visiting Japanese restaurants and BARs.

While he's been

expanding masu to BARs and other places in Vietnam,

I hadn't been interacting with shops in Cebu,

and I felt a sense of urgency about that.

Part of the reason was that my weekends were fully booked every day,

but even on weekdays, after classes end at 8 PM,

I have free time until the 10 PM curfew.

If I skip the vocabulary class,

I can free up 4 hours from 6 PM to 10 PM.

So this week, I'm going to visit BARs and restaurants run by Japanese people in Cebu.

The reason I was able to think this way

is because I saw my teammates'

energetic activities through social media,

and I was reminded once again how important it is to have teammates working alongside you.

By the way, here's his Vietnam activity log.

[*Free to read]

Kuma's Vietnam Experience | note

Now then, I'm going full throttle on weekday evenings during this final week in Cebu!!

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "Connecting at a BAR." Having a business talk in Japanese for the first time in a while. After classes and dinner at school, there's a vocabulary class from 7 PM, but
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"Connecting at a BAR."

▼ Having a Business Talk in Japanese for the First Time in a While

After classes and dinner at school,

there's a vocabulary class from 7 PM,

but today I skipped it and went out!

Memorizing English vocabulary is important,

but even more than that,

I want to do things during this final week

that can only be done while I'm in Cebu.

That means

getting Japanese people who run restaurants and BARs

to learn about FOMUS.

Up until now,

I'd been focusing on Masu Photo shoots,

and hadn't really been going to

shops where Japanese people hang out.

But "Kuma," who's also a FOMUS member,

is currently active in Vietnam,

making lots of connections,

and spreading FOMUS masu to multiple shops there.

When I heard about that,

I thought, "Wait, have I actually done any work to spread the FOMUS brand to restaurants and bars in Cebu??"

I'd been so absorbed in Masu Photo shoots

that I hadn't been

approaching restaurants at all.

So,

this week I headed to a Japanese BAR.

As I mentioned in a previous post,

I went to "BAR Ripple."

There's a person named "Akko" who designed this shop, runs esthetic and beauty businesses in Japan, and is doing a ton of business in Cebu as well.

This time, I got to have a proper, in-depth conversation with her.

*Last time, there were other people around and I could only talk a little

By coincidence, all the other customers were locals,

and I was the only one who came alone,

so I got to talk about FOMUS

and hear about Akko's story.

Akko is currently

also working as a consultant,

and when I told her about FOMUS,

she gave me all kinds of advice.

*That's pretty rare

From FOMUS accessories to regular masu,

I was able to reconfirm that there are lots of things

we can do to expand overseas.

As expected,

conversations with people who are seriously into business

are incredibly valuable and fun.

This time, we talked about the story behind masu

and what kind of value to attach to them.

I thought I was already doing that,

but she looked at things from a different perspective than mine

and shared her thoughts,

and everything she said made me think, "I see!"

When you get a different perspective, the advice you receive changes too.

It was a different approach from the advice I'd received before, and it was stimulating in a good way.

As always, when you take action yourself,

your connections with people grow.

And I gave Akko at BAR Ripple

a mini masu as a gift.

FOMUS masu are masu that connect the world.

Once again, I felt motivated to get moving even more!

And then,

since there's a curfew, I left the shop at 9:40 PM.

Walking back takes about 30 minutes,

and taxis weren't coming at all,

so I flagged down a motorcycle and rode it.

After 3 months,

this was my first time using a motorcycle.

I'm not sure about the going rate,

but I handed over 50 pesos, about 120 yen,

and they didn't say "Not enough!" so I guess it was fine!

Both ways on a motorcycle,

way cheaper than taking a taxi!

Glad I got to have this experience in my last week!

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "Meeting the Veterans." The final test. Before getting into the main topic, let me share what happened today. It's week 12, so today was the school's progress test.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"Meeting the Veterans."

▼ The Final Test

Before getting into the main topic, let me share what happened today.

It's week 12,

so today was the school's progress test.

They evaluate you on four skills.

I'm not hung up on test scores,

but getting a good score is always nice,

so I planned to take it seriously, but...

The questions were harder

than the previous two tests!!

For speaking, they give you a topic and you have to respond, but the explanations are difficult even in Japanese,

so having to answer in English

raises the bar significantly.

*The fact that the test difficulty varies makes me wonder if you can really judge by test scores

What I learned in these 3 months is that

I don't properly understand English grammar.

I can get my point across,

but my grammar is all over the place,

so apparently it's a bit hard to follow.

I'll study this properly

after I get back to Japan.

And with that,

the final progress test is done.

Scores will be announced next time!

▼ Following Google Maps to Get There

Now, as I wrote in the previous post,

this week is all about going out!

I've been showing up at various places.

Following yesterday's visit to Ripple,

I went to "Naoki's Dining Bar,"

which is owned by a Japanese person.

I found this place

by searching "BAR" on Google Maps.

They also serve food,

so I had dinner there too!

I met the owner, Naoki, for the first time.

He's turning 69 this year (2024), amazingly.

A real veteran who's been in Cebu for 35 years.

He was already in Cebu

before I was even born.

Naoki and I talked about business and life.

In particular,

"Life is only once, so enjoy the present"

is something I already understand,

but when a businessman much older than me says it,

it really straightens your spine.

There were also lots of regulars,

and I got to hear various stories from them too.

FOMUS was a big hit there as well.

Once again, I realized that when you take action,

some kind of result will always come from it.

BARs like this are great for chatting with friends,

but if you want to really talk with the owner, going alone is best.

This might come from my experience as a traveler.

I truly had a fruitful time.

Also, the girl working at the shop

kept pouring me beers,

asking "Another beer?",

and since I kept saying yes, I ended up having 4! lol

That's how much fun I was having, I guess.

On the way back, it was almost curfew,

so I caught a motorcycle and headed back to school!

I was just minutes away from being late. lol

After that, I went up to the school rooftop

and did a little running.

In September, there's a running-while-drinking event

waiting for me in France,

so I tried running with that in mind,

but it was pretty tough!

I felt like I really need to train more!

title_en: Three Months in the Blink of an Eye
excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "Three Months in the Blink of an Eye." The end is here. It's day 82 of my Cebu stay. When you put it in numbers, it's surprisingly big. Now, three months of language school ends tomorrow.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"Three Months in the Blink of an Eye."

▼ The End Is Here

It's day 82 of my Cebu stay.

When you put it in numbers, it's surprisingly big.

Now, three months of language school ends tomorrow.

Plus, tomorrow (Friday) there are no afternoon classes,

so effectively, today was the last afternoon class.

After class, I snapped some photos with the teachers.

Dinner was probably my last meal in the cafeteria.

After that, I went to special class.

The class where we study 20 vocabulary words every day is also over.

Then I did a little work,

and from 9 PM, spent just one hour at the closest BAR to school.

I've been here so many times that the owner remembers me and even knows my graduation date. lol

Today, I wanted a drink, but more than that,

I wanted more people to know about this wonderful place, so I gave them a FOMUS masu.

FOMUS masu exist across Japan, as well as in Taiwan, Malaysia, and Vietnam, and are now in 3 establishments in the Philippines.

I see it as one form of community -- creating an environment where friends and members who own masu can go and enjoy drinks there.

That's why

we place masu around the world,

but it's not just about placing them -- the meaning of placing masu only comes through when you interact with the local owners.

This time too, they happily accepted it.

Tomorrow's post will probably be a bit long,

so I'll keep this one short.

Plus, I also need to write my graduation speech! lol

Anyway,

my Cebu life is coming to an end!

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "Cebu Study Abroad Complete!" Three months that flew by. So, the time has finally come. My Cebu study abroad started on February 4th, and today, 2024/04/26, I officially graduated.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"Cebu Study Abroad Complete!"

▼ Three Months That Flew By

So, the time has finally come.

My Cebu study abroad started on February 4th,

and today, 2024/04/26, I officially graduated.

In a different way from my school days,

this was a deeply memorable graduation.

It was a short 3 months,

but besides improving my English,

enrolling in school as a working adult

was also a first for me,

and on top of that, I got to work with local models --

I was able to have experiences unlike anything before.

Being able to have these experiences

at this timing of being 29 years old

is huge for me.

▼ And Then, the Graduation Ceremony

I'd attended many friends' graduation ceremonies before, but this time I was the one graduating.

Nine people graduated on this day.

At the ceremony,

one or two people give a graduation speech,

and I got to speak as a representative.

The speech will be posted later

And I received my certificate of completion.

▼ Sports Day in the Afternoon

In the afternoon, there was a sports event at my school, CELLA.

Actually, CELLA has 2 campuses:

the Uni Campus and the Premium Campus.

It was a sports competition between the two campuses.

I didn't participate as a player

but went as a spectator.

It was fun chatting with other spectators

while watching the games.

Then, time to head back to school...

or so I thought, but...

Taxis wouldn't come even when I called! lol

Since it was a Friday night, taxis, Grab,

and jeepneys were all packed.

On my very last day,

I ended up walking 30 minutes back to school! lol

After that, I had my final dinner in the cafeteria,

then went drinking at a nearby BAR

with students and the Japanese manager.

After that, other students came to join us.

In the end, from 7 PM to 11:30 PM,

we had fun for about 4 hours.

After that, we talked on the school rooftop

with students until past 1 AM!

Right until the very end,

I enjoyed my time in Cebu to the fullest.

Once again, these 3 months in Cebu

became a time of great experience and growth for me.

In expanding the FOMUS brand,

I painfully realized in 2023 that I, as the founder,

need to level up myself for it to grow further.

That includes English ability,

and also the ability to create bigger budgets

and pull in funding.

During these 3 months,

I was able to improve my English, interact with locals,

and gain experience using English to secure funding,

among many other things.

Honestly, it was an intense 3 months,

but if I hadn't come to Cebu at this timing,

there were so many encounters I would have missed.

It must be some kind of fate.

These connections aren't just limited to here --

I believe they'll reconnect somewhere in the world.

In the Philippines, over 40 people already

own FOMUS masu.

I'll continue working to create new connections

through FOMUS masu around the world.

And with that, starting tomorrow it's the Manila chapter!!

Stay tuned!

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "Manila Chapter: Masu Photo Shoot." The time to leave Cebu! My 3-month stay in Cebu has come to an end. It was truly a time filled with so many memories and learnings.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"Manila Chapter: Masu Photo Shoot."

▼ The Time to Leave Cebu!

My 3-month stay in Cebu has come to an end.

It was truly a time filled with so many memories and learnings.

Honestly, I have no regrets.

So, packing time in the morning.

As an address hopper,

packing a suitcase is second nature to me.

All my belongings packed up in about 15 minutes.

I had a little extra time,

so I went to the school rooftop to prepare for my Manila work.

And then, departure time!

The Japanese women I'd gone to see Cebu's stunning views with

came to see me off.

So grateful!

Then, off to Mactan Island airport.

Checked my bags, got through security,

everything was going smoothly, but then

the flight was delayed by about 15 minutes.

The 12:20 departure became 12:35.

Arrival was supposed to be at 2 PM,

but we ended up arriving at 2:50 PM.

The Manila shoot was scheduled

for 3:30 PM on the hotel rooftop, so the schedule got unexpectedly tight.

*Would have been fine without the delay

Since I'd checked my suitcase too,

I was thinking, "There's no way I'll make it," but then

miraculously, my suitcase was the first one

to come out on the conveyor belt!

I was able to leave the airport without waiting at all.

Then,

I needed to find a taxi from there,

and lots of touts tried to grab me.

Since I'd been in Cebu for 3 months,

I already had a sense of the budget.

I grabbed a public taxi

and headed to the hotel.

Still, it was about

1.5 times more expensive than Cebu taxis.

Since I was in a rush,

I used the expressway too.

And I arrived at the hotel at 3:35 PM.

Here, I met up with FOMUS member Kuma.

Plus, the models had already arrived.

So we started shooting right away.

Got some great Masu Photos.

The second and third models

were waiting on the hotel rooftop where we'd originally planned to shoot.

Snapped some shots here too.

Being able to take photos like these

was only possible because FOMUS member Kuma had joined.

After the shoot,

we went to a nearby cafe with 2 of the models.

The four of us chatted.

This kind of interaction is

what FOMUS values the most.

We were also able to connect models with each other.

Being able to do things like this

is because the FOMUS brand has been gradually growing.

And because my English has improved.

Today I had lots of conversations with the models, and the English conversation was very smooth.

Even Kuma sitting next to me was impressed.

That's proof of how seriously I took

those 3 months of English immersion.

After parting ways at the cafe,

we headed out.

We went to get dinner.

A highly-rated local restaurant on Google Maps.

It was incredibly delicious!

After that, we went to a BAR run by a Japanese-Filipino couple.

Here too,

we got lots of information about Manila.

Of course, the conversation here was in English too.

And we had some great talks.

After that, we went to a nearby sake BAR.

*Not because I wanted sake, but because I wanted to see what price range they were selling at.

A bottle that costs about 1,500 yen in Japan

was selling for 1,500 yen per glass,

and the other customers besides us were from other countries.

Probably Chinese or Korean.

I know prices go up with imports,

but still, that's quite a price.

I'm imagining that running a small FOMUS BAR overseas in the future

could be really interesting.

*A place where overseas guests and Japanese people mix would be wonderful

And so,

it was a day where shooting started

immediately after arriving in Manila.

I'll enjoy the remaining 3 days

while continuing to improve my English and deliver FOMUS masu!

title_en: The Joy of Having Friends Around the World
excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "The Joy of Having Friends Around the World." Masu Photo shoots continue! Manila, day 2. We're staying in Makati, which is in the city next to Manila.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"The Joy of Having Friends Around the World."

▼ Masu Photo Shoots Continue!

Manila, day 2.

We're staying in

Makati, which is in the city next to Manila.

From there, it's about a 20-minute drive to Manila.

At Rizal Park, one of Manila's tourist spots,

we did a Masu Photo shoot.

Originally, we had planned to shoot 3 people,

but 2 cancelled last minute,

so it ended up being just 1 person.

The person we photographed this time

is someone who's also active as an idol in Manila.

She said she'll be participating in events in Japan this year!

Masu Photo shoots attract some unique people, unlike regular shoots.

*Holding a masu for a photo shoot is already pretty unique in itself

Since we'd planned for 3 but ended up with 1,

after the shoot we chatted at a nearby Starbucks.

I checked out her YouTube and other content.

I was thinking it'd be fun if someday she could do FOMUS character "KUKU" cosplay and dance.

We parted at 10:30 AM,

and we decided to check out the tourist spots near Rizal Park.

Nearby is a relative term since it's about a 20-minute walk, so we wanted to take a shortcut.

Then, by chance,

a two-seater go-kart came along,

and we negotiated.

"30 minutes, 350 pesos per person, and I'll show you the tourist spots!"

they said, but we only needed a ride to one place since the rest was within a 5-minute walk, so I haggled.

350 pesos is the tourist price,

but I'd already been in the Philippines for 3 months,

so negotiating like this is second nature.

Negotiated down to 50 pesos each, 100 pesos total. Success.

*Proudly showing off my English negotiation skills

Then, to Manila Cathedral.

It was an absolutely massive building.

Since it was Sunday, lots of people were visiting.

I checked out the souvenir section,

but it was all religious figurines,

and I wondered if anyone actually buys these.

Then, to Fort Santiago nearby.

This is a place that was occupied by the Japanese during the war.

Apparently, many Filipinos were killed by the Japanese there.

It was a place with such historical weight.

Then, lunch.

For lunch, I met up with Yi Sun,

my Chinese roommate from the language school in Cebu.

We reunited after about a week! lol

He lives in Manila.

We had Chinese hot pot.

We ordered so much

that it turned into a food challenge,

but we finished it all.

After that, everyone went to

"SM MOA," one of the biggest malls in Asia.

It was absolutely massive, and packed with people.

It easily surpasses Japanese malls.

What I feel coming to Manila is that

while Japan is stagnating, other countries keep advancing.

The average age in the Philippines is 24,

and the mall was full of relatively young people.

Going forward, Japan will have more elderly people,

and it's clear that national power will decline,

while other countries move ahead.

That's what I felt since coming to Manila.

There was even a

merry-go-round inside the mall! lol

The kids were having a great time.

After that, we went to Yi Sun's recommended sunset spot.

The coastline was beautiful.

Then, in the evening, we said goodbye to Yi Sun

and headed back to the hotel.

After that, we bought drinks at a nearby LAWSON

and ate Chinese food near the hotel.

It was way too spicy and I struggled.

Finally, we relaxed in the hotel sauna.

What I felt once again is that

having friends and teammates around the world

makes life so much more enjoyable.

This time, through Masu Photo shoots,

I became friends with an idol active in the Philippines,

and I had lunch with a friend I made at language school.

Being able to connect with people

in unfamiliar places and lands like this

is incredibly fulfilling,

and it's an experience you can never have

without taking initiative yourself.

Everything that happened today

was the result of being proactive.

Anyway, only a few days left of activities in Cebu!

I'll keep making the most of my time!

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "To the Slum: Happyland." I want to see it with my own eyes. Manila, day 3. I went to "Happyland" in the Tondo district, a slum area close to the center of Manila.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"To the Slum: Happyland."

▼ I Want to See It with My Own Eyes

Manila, day 3.

I went to "Happyland" in the Tondo district,

a slum area close to the center of Manila.

It's said to be a danger zone

that even locals won't set foot in,

but I wanted to see it with my own eyes,

so I went.

About 30 minutes by Grab from our hotel in Makati.

The moment we entered the Tondo district,

the scenery around us started to change.

And then, into Happyland.

The first shock was seeing

lots of children and adults living

in a place covered with garbage.

The kids were all smiling,

giving high-fives and talking to us.

In Japan, you'd be taught "Don't go near strangers," but here, they come right up to you.

Very friendly kids.

I also asked a local woman for directions,

and she didn't glare at us at all

and kindly helped us.

At this point,

my impression had completely changed.

And then, we went deeper.

There were absolutely zero tourists,

but we ventured into places where only locals would go.

This was really deep.

In narrow alleyways,

people peeling garlic, people sleeping,

kids playing in water,

adults cooking some kind of mystery meat.

Driven by curiosity, I went into places

that you probably shouldn't enter.

The smells and the width of the paths were completely different.

Some of the children born here

apparently aren't even registered with the Philippine government, making it difficult for them to live outside this area.

This is what the Philippines' major issue, "poverty," looks like.

There were also unexpected things.

Believe it or not,

kids in this district were playing online games

and sending messages through Facebook Messenger.

The internet is amazing --

even the children of the Tondo district can access information from around the world.

Also, regarding language,

since the kids can't go to school,

I saw adults teaching them language

right there.

In Japan, going to school is taken for granted,

but by global standards,

that's actually remarkable.

▼ What's "Normal" Isn't Normal at All

We can't choose where we're born.

Fortunately,

I was born in Japan

and have been living as if it's natural,

but I felt firsthand

just how privileged that is.

Deep inside Happyland,

young men were playing basketball barefoot.

That ball was probably donated by someone from around the world.

Thanks to lots of support,

the children of the Tondo district live with smiles.

Even without "money,"

there are plenty of "happy" people.

I witnessed that firsthand.

The poverty issue

is hard to understand from Japan.

That's because the level of poverty in Japan and the Philippines is completely different.

Every day, garbage is collected from the city,

and children sort through it

and sell it for a tiny amount of money.

With that money,

they buy and eat discarded food from the city

that's been washed and cooked.

What I saw this time was children eating what looked like grilled chicken and rice.

They were also selling meat and vegetables,

but flies were all over everything.

Clearly not hygienic.

Still,

things have probably improved a bit compared to the past.

"Money," "happiness," "environment" --

for me,

it was a chance to think about these things deeply once again.

▼ To the Most Developed Area

After leaving Happyland in the Tondo district,

taxis weren't coming at all...

But,

a jeepney happened to pass by, so we hopped on.

We rode as far as it would take us,

and ended up at a church in the same Tondo district.

I'd seen many churches

in the Philippines,

but this was an impressive building too.

Then, we headed to

a place called BGC.

This is a place with offices of companies from around the world

and lots of expats.

Happyland and BGC

look completely different.

Is this really the same country?

That's what I thought.

After arriving,

we went to "Abe," a famous Filipino restaurant.

The food here was incredibly delicious.

Then, we strolled down High Street.

So many shops everywhere.

Until 3 years ago, I lived

in Akasaka, Minato Ward,

and this felt like a bigger version of the garden at Tokyo Midtown.

There's also a big mall,

with floors that have the same luxury vibe as Midtown or Omotesando Hills.

Since companies and expats from around the world gather here,

everything is very well maintained.

Walking around and exploring,

I found art pieces everywhere.

I could see that it's already more vibrant and developed than Japan.

However, I also experienced firsthand

the enormous disparity in the Philippines.

*Both sides had smiles.

▼ Masu Photo Shoot Too

In the evening, I did a Masu Photo shoot with one model.

The venue was my hotel.

Not in the room, of course! lol

We shot in the lobby and lounge.

She's a trilingual who speaks Tagalog, English, and Korean.

Because she speaks Korean,

she works as a CEO's secretary.

She also teaches English to Koreans online!

Only 25 years old and already so impressive!!

She's also into Japanese traditional crafts,

and she'd studied up on FOMUS and me through Instagram and such.

We got some great Masu Photos this time too.

FOMUS masu models generally own a masu, so they can connect from anywhere in the world.

The tool that connects FOMUS members

around the world is the "masu."

The last night in Manila

was a truly meaningful time!

Well, tomorrow is the day I head home.

I'll enjoy the final day!

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "Thinking on Your Feet." Something unexpected happened. Manila, day 4. It's the final day of my activities in the Philippines. Due to the return flight schedule, I need to be at the airport by noon, but
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"Thinking on Your Feet."

▼ Something Unexpected Happened

Manila, day 4.

It's the final day of my activities in the Philippines.

Because of my return flight,

I need to be at the airport

by noon,

but I have 3 Masu Photo shoots scheduled.

Learning from past experiences,

I'd already contacted everyone the day before

and told them the time and place.

I'd planned to start shooting at 8 AM,

so while eating breakfast at the hotel at 7, I got replies.

One said, "I caught a cold."

Another said, with no reason given, "I can't make it."

Yeah, yeah.

I'm used to it by now. lol

A cancellation or two like that

doesn't make me angry anymore.

I figured it'd be just one person, but

the last one wasn't responding either.

When that happens,

I need to rethink my schedule.

It was confirmed that I could move around

from 8 AM to 11 AM,

but no model was coming.

So I could either stay at the hotel

or go out.

So,

I prepared two options.

Option 1: Shoot happens

If I hear back by 8:30 AM,

we'll shoot from 9 AM.

Option 2: No shoot

If I don't hear from the model by 8:30 AM,

cancel the shoot.

Then go to the National Museum of the Philippines,

which opens at 9 AM.

*Apparently it's huge and famous

*And it's free

I made this decision during breakfast.

By 8 AM, I was fully ready.

In the end, no reply came.

So,

off to the National Museum of the Philippines by Grab!!

GO!!

Honestly, with over 30 Masu Photos already shot,

I had more than enough material. lol

About 30 minutes by Grab.

Arrived at the National Museum.

Got there by 9:15 AM.

Foreigners need to show their passport,

and all bags must be checked in,

but that worked out perfectly this time!

Because the security guard would watch

my suitcase and belongings!

So I went through the museum empty-handed.

This is

a cultural institution that academically researches, documents, preserves, exhibits, and fosters Filipino arts, anthropology, archaeology, botany, geology, zoology, and more.

Besides works by the Philippines' leading artists, it exhibits sculptures and drawings by national hero Jose Rizal.

And it's all free!!

So,

for my last activity in the Philippines, I enjoyed the National Museum!!

Since FOMUS has a digital art exhibition starting

right after I return to Japan,

being able to see a large-scale art exhibition

at this timing was great.

The display methods were also good reference.

And the sheer number of works was incredible! lol

It goes up to the 4th floor with a crazy number of rooms!

Even going through super quickly, it took about an hour and a half.

Art really is fascinating.

I care about the content of the works,

but I also love the frames,

so I end up thinking, "Why did they choose this frame for this piece?"

Looking at the works, you can feel

that there are no right answers in art.

That's what makes it interesting.

For the art exhibition starting May 3-4,

I want visitors to enjoy it,

so I'll start preparing right after I get home.

*No time to rest after getting home

After enjoying the museum,

it was time to head to the airport.

Here, I also said goodbye to FOMUS member Kuma.

He and I were on different flights,

each heading back to Japan separately.

I was flying Cebu Pacific,

but the baggage check-in line was insanely long.

I waited about an hour and a half

just to check in.

So my plan to eat lunch was completely ruined. lol

I ended up ordering in-flight food.

First time buying snacks in a while! lol

And just like that, I made it safely back to Japan.

About 3 months.

Studying English, working, making connections, visiting slums.

Everything was stimulating!!

Especially, the main objective of

improving my English

was successfully achieved.

Of course, you can't speak perfectly

in just 3 months,

I knew that before I went.

But through this intensive training,

my mental block with English was removed,

and communicating in English

became so much easier.

Thanks to that, conversations with local models

became really smooth.

Being able to use English

instantly expanded my world even further.

Up until now, I've traveled to 15 countries,

but that was all just getting by with broken English, and I hardly interacted with locals at all.

But my world travels from here on will be different.

Not just sightseeing,

but also enjoying interactions with local people.

And even in Japan,

there are plenty of foreigners in tourist areas.

I can now interact with them

more than ever before.

3 months.

Just by being able to use a little English,

my way of thinking changed this much.

And I feel like I've become more cheerful than ever.

Maybe that's the influence of the Filipino people.

Watching how cheerful they are,

you really notice how shy Japanese people are. lol

*Including me

These 3 months in the Philippines truly

became a fulfilling time for me.

Grateful to everyone who was part of it!!

I'm writing a summary in the next post,

so please check that out too!!

title_en: Summary: Wrapping Up 3 Months of Activities in the Philippines
excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "Summary: Wrapping Up 3 Months of Activities in the Philippines." Background. I'm writing this article on the return flight. My activities in the Philippines, which started on February 4, 2024,
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"Summary: Wrapping Up 3 Months of Activities in the Philippines."

▼ Background

I'm writing this article on the return flight.

My activities in the Philippines,

which started on February 4, 2024,

have successfully concluded today, April 30.

As I wrote in the very first article,

these 3 months were

mainly about improving my own English ability.

As I'm about to enter my 30s,

it's going to get harder to only work with Japanese people, and for FOMUS, which I lead,

language barriers will inevitably arise

as we expand globally.

So I figured, before I turn 30,

I should get my English up to speed,

and spend the remaining 70 years not just in Japan,

but as someone who can thrive on the world stage --

that was one of the triggers.

Also, last year (2023),

I went to Taiwan and Malaysia for work.

In Taiwan, GPS runner Shimizu

was fluent in English and interacting with Taiwanese people right next to me, and not being able to speak English, I felt frustrated that I wanted to connect more but couldn't.

Furthermore, when I visited Malaysia alone

to do "Masu Photo" activities --

taking photos with locals holding FOMUS masu --

the initial contact through SNS was fine,

but once we met in person,

I had to rely on my own English ability.

Here too, the language barrier meant

I wanted to talk more, but because I couldn't, the other person would hold back too.

So, when I asked a fluent Japanese person I met in Malaysia, "What should I do?",

they advised me, "If you want to learn English in a short time, you should do a language study abroad."

From there, I researched right away

and decided on this Cebu language study abroad.

Originally I planned for 2 months,

but "you need at least 3 months for it to stick"

was what I read online, so I went with 3 months.

That's the background of how

I ended up doing a language study abroad in Cebu.

▼ Aiming to Kill Three Birds with One Stone

The decision to study abroad was made.

At this point, I was already 28.

And I had my own company and businesses.

My work mainly consists of:

- Photographer

- Masu brand "FOMUS"

- AI instructor

If I add "English" to these skills and strengths,

my target audience expands dramatically.

Plus, I could do Masu Photo -- taking photos with local models holding masu -- on site.

*Already proven in Malaysia

So,

I figured I could expand

the masu brand "FOMUS"

while staying in Cebu.

Since I can express the expansion through photography,

I could also be active as a photographer.

Furthermore, as an AI instructor,

I work with image generation AI and other tools,

and right after returning, on May 3-4,

FOMUS is hosting a digital art exhibition.

That means I could even create works

using AI together with local people.

Masu, cameras, and AI -- all could be utilized.

In this way,

I decided to leverage the strengths

that I and FOMUS have during the 3-month stay.

▼ The 3-Month Language Study Abroad Begins

Now, here's where the study abroad story starts.

I spent 3 months at

"CELLA UNI campus" in Talamban, Cebu.

It's a dormitory-style school

with classrooms, bedrooms,

and a cafeteria all in the same building.

In other words,

you don't need to go outside for classes.

You can choose from a single room to a quad room,

and naturally, the single room is the most expensive.

I chose the quad room because

"I wanted to save money" and

"sharing a room means more opportunities to speak English."

Before coming to the Philippines, I'd been an address hopper traveling around Japan, and I'd stayed in dormitory rooms too, so having other people around when sleeping doesn't really bother me anymore. lol

*Looking back after 3 months, I'm glad I chose the quad room. I had roommates from other countries and got more chances to speak English, and we ate together and went out together. The level of friendship you develop is also different.

The only weakness is that there's one bathroom, toilet, and shower shared, so when one person is using it, the others have to wait.

If that doesn't bother you,

I recommend the quad room.

Now.

The school has several courses,

and I chose "Power Speaking 2."

This is 8 classes per day:

6 one-on-one classes with a teacher,

and 2 group classes.

*45 minutes per class

"Power Speaking 1" splits it 4 and 4.

*More group classes means slightly cheaper.

Since I knew for sure I'd be speaking English more in business settings, I chose the course with more one-on-one classes.

After actually spending 3 months, what I learned is that

even in one-on-one classes, they use textbooks,

so it's not that different from classes in Japan.

English skills are:

- Speaking

- Listening

- Writing

- Reading

These four.

Since I

didn't need to raise my English test scores,

I abandoned reading and writing

and focused specifically on speaking and listening.

Starting from week 2,

I asked my teachers, "Please don't use textbooks and focus on conversation instead."

I wanted to do things that

could only be done in Cebu,

so I wanted to improve my English skills

through conversations with Filipino teachers.

Of course, learning from textbooks is important too.

You learn a lot of idioms from textbooks.

Also, at this school, you could

change teachers every week,

so if I felt even slightly incompatible,

I'd swap right away.

During the first 3 weeks,

I listed teacher info

and checked the slots I wanted to change against teachers' open slots.

If they matched, I could switch.

I probably changed teachers the most out of anyone.

Also, there was a time when a teacher resigned but hid it,

and the next week an unknown teacher replaced them,

and that teacher didn't click with me at all.

That whole week, just that one time slot was a downer.

So that's how it went with

class slots, style, and room arrangements.

And,

how much did my level improve in 3 months?

Honestly, my level test results

didn't improve that much.

Out of 80 points,

my first level test was 26 points.

After 1 month: 40 points

After 2 months: 44 points

After 3 months: 40 points

It actually went down at the end. lol

Looking at just these scores,

you might think studying abroad is pointless, but there are things about English that can't be measured by scores.

That's communication ability.

When you spend 3 months conversing in English,

your ears get used to it, and you pick up what you need for conversation.

So while my scores didn't go up much,

my ability to speak and listen to English has improved.

Where that became most clearly evident was

during shoots with masu models, when I could truly feel my English had improved.

▼ Three Months of Non-Stop Masu Photos

On weekdays, I'm immersed in English from morning to night,

so I basically don't go out.

On school days off, of course I can go out.

In my case, I focused on English studies on weekdays,

and on days off, I practiced what I learned in class

through conversations with masu models --

a schedule with clear input and output.

Other students went

island hopping, snorkeling,

swimming with whale sharks,

or went to other islands for activities with friends, but I spent my time at Cebu shopping malls doing shoots nonstop.

Especially in February, from the very first week after arriving,

I went all out with Masu Photos.

Thanks to that, I shot 6 models in the first week

and made connections too.

At that time, I was desperately listening to English

and communicating.

Models' English and teachers' English

have different speeds, so my brain was running at full capacity! lol

In February, I was shooting with someone almost every week.

Then in March,

I made time to hang out with

people I'd become close with at school.

That's important too.

At the end of March, during Holy Week,

there were 4 consecutive days off,

and I went to "Zamboanga," a place nobody else would go.

Zamboanga is on Mindanao Island,

south of Cebu,

about 1 hour from Cebu by plane.

I went alone because it's easier to move around solo.

When I got there, there were zero tourists,

and Grab didn't even work there...

So I got around by riding "tricycles" --

modified motorcycles.

Zamboanga was only four days,

but I shot with 4 models.

After some of the shoots, we went to cafes

and hung out.

Here too, I was able to

communicate what I wanted in English,

and it was an experience where I felt my English improving firsthand.

Going to Zamboanga was

really, really worth it.

*Later, people in Cebu and Manila would find it amusing that I'd been to Zamboanga

Then, April.

This is when I did shoots with

professional models, which I'd been targeting since February.

Actually, since February, I'd been sending massive amounts of messages on Instagram to Filipino models asking them to be Masu Photo models.

I messaged about 200 people,

but only got 8 replies. And not under great conditions.

Honestly, I thought it wasn't going to happen.

But being the persistent person I am,

I decided to change my approach.

There were twin models I really wanted to shoot with,

so I sent them a new message.

This time, I changed the pitch from

being a Masu Photo model to

becoming an ambassador for the accessory brand "FOMUS Parure."

In other words,

I made the brand ambassador appointment a perk.

As a result, they replied,

and after negotiating, the shoot happened.

With 7.7 million TikTok followers,

these amazing twins turned out to be only 19 years old.

They're models with an exciting future ahead.

And there were other professional shoots too.

I'd been telling teachers about

wanting to shoot with pros,

and a teacher said their friend and relative are models

and offered to introduce them.

Plus, 2 models I'd selected from the mass messages

joined in,

making it a shoot with 6 models total.

For the professional shoots,

we paid for promotion costs and talent fees.

We also used a hotel beach,

and FOMUS covered those costs too.

Paying to shoot Masu Photos was something

I'd never done even in Japan,

so it was a first.

From a promotional standpoint,

the twin shoot was great,

but the teachers' friends and the models I selected

were a bit weak from a promotional perspective. Because they didn't have much influence.

In that case, it might have been better to do mutual free shoots, like with models who wanted to do Masu Photo anyway.

Through these experiences,

I realized something big.

That is, there are two types of Masu Photo.

One is shooting with local models who want to take photos holding masu.

The standard Masu Photo.

The merits are:

- You shoot with people who are interested in FOMUS and masu

- The connection after the shoot deepens (a friendship feeling)

- No cost

The demerits are:

- Low influence means the brand doesn't spread much

And the other type of Masu Photo is

professional Masu Photo.

The merits are:

- High influence leads to brand awareness expansion

- Brand value increases

*People see these models and want to participate in Masu Photo too

The demerits are:

- Since it's a business Masu Photo, it's more of a business partnership than friendship

- They don't really feel like part of the FOMUS community

- There are costs involved

That's the gist.

In other words,

Masu Photo has two types:

the traditional "Masu Photo" that creates

connections with local subjects, and

the "Masu Photo" taken for brand value and reach.

I learned that I need to differentiate

Masu Photo depending on the purpose.

That's a huge takeaway!

▼ Plenty of Other Learnings Too

Masu Photo wasn't the only learning.

I also played FOMUS's card game "SILVA" during class.

https://silva.official.ec/

It was my first time playing SILVA with people from overseas,

and once you learn the rules, it's a game that doesn't need any language barrier.

So the question was: can I teach the rules in English

and then actually battle?

Turns out, it worked perfectly!!

In a 45-minute class,

I used about 25 minutes for explanation

and the remaining 20 minutes for battling

with commentary along the way.

Also, because the design is

really cool,

one teacher bought SILVA and plays it with friends on days off.

This proved that SILVA is also

content that can go global.

If there's one regret,

it's that I couldn't do a 4-player match

with several teachers.

I'll do that another time.

And AI activities too.

I created artwork with teachers using image generation AI.

You might wonder how that topic comes up, but since my classes were all conversation-based, the teachers would ask about my work and personal life.

So they naturally dig into what I can do.

For me too, having to explain myself in English is great practice.

When I talked about AI,

they were really interested, and that's how it came about.

I didn't see anyone in the Philippines using AI well, so there's probably still an information gap.

Well, it's the same in Japan too.

So,

I'm feeling like teaching AI overseas could be an option.

Actually, with one teacher,

we talked about me teaching AI lessons after I return to Japan

while the teacher teaches me English.

For me, I get to study English

and also talk about AI in English,

and if that works out, I could teach people from other countries about AI in English too.

I'm really looking forward to that.

▼ FOMUS Masu in BARs Too!

I also got shops to display FOMUS masu.

In February and March, I was so focused on Masu Photo

that I forgot about finding shops to place FOMUS masu.

When a FOMUS teammate went to Vietnam in April

and did personal activities

while also spreading FOMUS masu,

scouting out shops one after another

and getting them to display masu,

seeing that made me think, "I need to do this too!!"

And that feeling drove me

to get masu placed in

3 shops during the final week.

These shops are places I'd already visited,

so someday when a FOMUS member with a masu

happens to be nearby, I'd love for them to visit.

In Japan, there's already a flow where people who know FOMUS or me visit shops or people that have FOMUS masu, so instead of keeping this just within Japan, we're expanding it worldwide.

▼ How Did I Spend My Free Time?

Honestly, there wasn't a single day I could just zone out.

That's how busy and fulfilling

these intense 3 months were.

In Japan,

I had time to watch anime,

but with English immersion from 7 AM

and classes not ending until 8 PM,

plus FOMUS work after that,

there was no free time.

In the first place, Amazon Prime Video doesn't work in the Philippines, so anime was forcibly off the table.

My one pleasure was

the weekly "Zoids" anime on YouTube.

Honestly, refreshing is important.

So once a week,

I went to a spa/massage place in Cebu.

Southeast Asian massages are

so much cheaper than in Japan,

and I go often.

Dry massage, oil massage,

aromatherapy --

I tried all kinds of massages.

My stress relief for 3 months

was Zoids and massages. lol

▼ Habits I Formed

Also,

this might be different from refreshing,

but at school,

I used the treadmill to run.

With my 30s approaching,

I didn't want my stamina to drop and work efficiency to suffer,

plus I'd decided to participate in the Medoc Marathon in France this September, and I wouldn't be able to run 42 km on the day without practice.

Also, in Japan I walked 10,000 steps every day,

but in Cebu the roads outside were narrow

and bumpy,

making it hard to walk, and with barely any commuting in my life,

I proactively exercised.

At first, even 3 km was tough,

but by the second half, I was running 5-9 km.

The body is your capital,

so I was able to stay on top of health management.

I also made early rising a habit.

This was simply because

I took the optional vocabulary class at 7 AM and 7 PM.

It was for those who wanted it,

and I took this class for 2 months.

Thanks to that, I started waking up at 6 AM

to review vocabulary.

As a result, in March,

I had the best vocabulary test scores

out of all the students

and even got an award for it! lol

As for exercise,

with the Medoc Marathon coming up,

I'll keep it going.

But early rising -- we'll see what happens. lol

If I can make waking up early and running a habit,

both should stick.

▼ Made Lots of Friends

And regarding friendships,

there were just so many encounters

that came specifically from studying abroad.

After becoming a working adult,

I never expected to make

this many friends.

Philippines, China, Korea, Vietnam, Taiwan, Japan --

I mainly made friends from these 6 countries.

Ages ranged mostly from 18 to 26.

With friends,

I went island hopping, snorkeling,

karaoke, and even clubbing.

Since I started my own business,

I'd been mostly hanging out with older people,

so making friends younger than me

who I could relate to outside of work was really nice.

Also, since I made friends in each country,

next time I visit those countries,

I might be able to reach out and meet up.

With SNS, you can always see what someone is up to.

I've exchanged Instagram with the people I met

during this study abroad,

and tons of them already watch my stories.

So lately, my posts include both English and Japanese.

Since I want to work overseas,

adding English to my SNS was a good move.

*Even if it means losing some Japanese followers

And the teachers too.

The teachers also felt like friends.

The teachers put a lot of thought

into my classes.

Because they had to do it without textbooks.

That's why conversation was the main thing,

and there was almost zero "class" feeling.

Thanks to that, it was easy to close the distance,

and I even went out drinking with teachers.

One thing I was careful about with friendships:

not clustering too much.

I noticed in the cafeteria that

people from the same country tend to stick together.

I don't like that comfort zone,

so I avoided grouping with the same people.

Also, some people got too close

and their relationships soured.

I didn't want to waste time on that,

so I kept a good distance.

That's why I basically

ate with my international friends.

Especially my roommates, who were all from different countries --

that was the most comfortable. lol

Also, during the last 2 weeks,

there were many Japanese working women,

and on weekdays we talked a lot.

Everyone was thinking about going to other countries after Cebu,

and I really clicked with people who had a global perspective.

Once again, I was able to become friends

with so many people from so many countries.

This is something money can't buy,

and it's for a lifetime.

And at the school graduation ceremony,

I gave a speech as the representative.

It was actually my first speech in English,

but I quite enjoyed it.

This photo was taken by a friend.

They filmed it on video.

▼ The Manila Chapter Was Full of Learnings Too

After the language study abroad in Cebu,

I went to Manila.

Within 30 minutes of arriving at the airport,

a shoot with 3 models

was already starting -- that kind of schedule.

Having done overseas Masu Photo so many times before, it started very smoothly,

and English communication felt even easier,

likely thanks to the 3 months of training.

Having FOMUS member Kuma

witness this also proves

that my communication with overseas models was smooth. lol

The biggest takeaway from Manila

was probably visiting the Happyland slum.

A place where not only tourists

but even Filipinos rarely go.

The public face of the Philippines as a developing nation shows

incredibly huge shopping malls,

BGC where global company offices gather, and other very clean and modern areas.

However, there are still mountains of unresolved issues.

One of them is "poverty."

Lots of children sort through garbage

brought in by trucks

and sell it for money.

There are also mountains of garbage everywhere.

The sanitation is clearly poor.

Yet the children

live with smiles on their faces.

Even when an outsider like me shows up,

they don't glare or beg for money.

They just high-five

or ask your name.

I'm not really great with kids honestly.

I don't know why.

But the children living in the slum

somehow looked adorable to me.

Maybe because they're living so earnestly.

I want to put this into words, but I can't right now...

Seeing the slum with my own eyes was a major experience, and it made me think more deeply about "money," "happiness," and "environment."

I'd always taken being born Japanese

for granted,

but it's actually miraculous.

If born into a different environment,

I'd be living a completely different life.

I became grateful

for being born in Japan.

That's how big the poverty problem is,

and though I was only there for 2 hours,

I felt it in my bones.

Next time I come, I'm thinking it'd be great

if FOMUS could provide some kind of support.

▼ All in All, It Was Amazing

I'll be arriving in Japan soon.

Time to wrap up this article.

The roughly 90 posts of my Cebu activity log

written here are what I experienced.

Because every day was packed with input,

I was able to produce output too.

Even on unremarkable weekdays,

I was always taking in some kind of information.

Days off go without saying.

As for English ability, I can confidently say it improved.

Of course, I can't speak fluently,

and I can't understand everything yet.

But for having work conversations,

I was able to get to a level

where it's not a major issue.

From here, it's about learning more vocabulary

and continuing to practice English daily.

In 6 weeks, I'll be living in America, Spain, and Ireland.

If I forget my English during that time,

these 3 months would lose half their meaning. lol

I aim to be fluent in English by 30,

and spend my 30s working hard with people overseas.

One more year. I'll keep working on improving my English.

What lies beyond that

is the global expansion of the masu brand "FOMUS."

I believe a brand can never grow bigger than its founder.

That's why I have to become stronger.

In this Philippines experience,

that became abundantly clear.

Before the content itself,

people are looking at my character.

I truly gained so many experiences.

In 2024, I recognized the need for English,

and through studying abroad, I gained so many learnings.

I'll use these experiences to soar even higher.

Thank you so much for reading this far!!

FOMUS MaSU
